Marie Curie was a Polish-French physicist and chemist who conducted pioneering research on radioactivity. Curie became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, the first person to win Nobel Prizes in two scientific fields, and the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two distinct sciences.

She and her husband, Pierre Curie, discovered polonium and radium in Paris in 1898. The couple worked together at the Sorbonne. Pierre died in 1906, after which Marie continued their research alone.

In 1934, Marie Curie died in Sancellemoz, France, from aplastic anemia caused by exposure to radiation.
